title: [LICM] Reassociate add/sub expressions to hoist invariant computations
author: SavchenkoValeriy
description:
While `sub` is not associative, we can still reassociate `add` and `sub`.

## Alive2 proofs

| Case | Transform | Proof |
|------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | `(x + c1) - c2` => `x + (c1 - c2)` | [proof](https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/iofzYy) |
| 2 | `(x - c1) - c2` => `x - (c1 + c2)` | [proof](https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/U4K_tE) |
| 3 | `(x - c1) + c2` => `x + (c2 - c1)` | [proof](https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/moiJVw) |

tests.test_body:
define void @sub_add_comm(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.sub = sub i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = add i64 %c2, %step.sub
  br label %loop
}

define void @sub_sub_two_uses(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 1000, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.sub = sub i64 %index, %c1
  call void @use(i64 %step.sub)
  %index.next = sub i64 %step.sub, %c2
  br label %loop
}

declare void @use()

define void @add_sub(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.add = add i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = sub i64 %step.add, %c2
  br label %loop
}

define void @add_sub_comm(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.add = add i64 %c1, %index
  %index.next = sub i64 %step.add, %c2
  br label %loop
}

define void @sub_sub(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.add = sub i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = sub i64 %step.add, %c2
  br label %loop
}

define void @sub_sub_drop_flags(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 1000, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.sub = sub nuw nsw i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = sub nuw nsw i64 %step.sub, %c2
  br label %loop
}

define void @sub_sub_variant_rhs_neg(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.sub = sub i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = sub i64 %c2, %step.sub
  br label %loop
}

define void @sub_add(i64 %c1, i64 %c2) {
entry:
  br label %loop

loop:                                             ; preds = %loop, %entry
  %index = phi i64 [ 0, %entry ], [ %index.next, %loop ]
  %step.sub = sub i64 %index, %c1
  %index.next = add i64 %step.sub, %c2
  br label %loop
}

comments.author: SavchenkoValeriy
comments.body:
> LGTM
> 
> There is a regression on llvm-opt-benchmark that may be worth a look.

@nikic great catch! I missed it.

I looked into it a bit and at least for that case I can minimize it to this:
```llvm
declare ptr @memchr(ptr, i32, i64)

define i64 @test(i64 %n) {
entry:
  %guard = icmp eq i64 %n, 0
  br i1 %guard, label %exit, label %loop

loop:
  %iv = phi i64 [ 1, %entry ], [ %next, %body ]
  %sub = sub nuw i64 %iv, %n
  %len = add i64 %sub, 1
  %len.zero = icmp eq i64 %len, 0
  br i1 %len.zero, label %exit, label %body

body:
  %p = call ptr @memchr(ptr null, i32 0, i64 %len)
  %next = ptrtoint ptr %p to i64
  br label %loop

exit:
  ret i64 0
}
```

The main problem is that we reassociate `(A - B) + C` into `A + (C - B)` and `A - B` has `nuw` giving us a good foundation for range-based reasoning. In the new expression, we can't generally infer the same flags and, thus, lose this opportunity further down the line.

I tried to experiment with inference of ranges for the original expression so that I can potentially attach it as `!range` to the new expression, but it didn't quite work this way.

What do you think we should do about it? Thanks!
